title: NOC 65320 – Dry cleaning, laundry and related occupations | GTR Immigration
description: NOC 65320 encompasses the skilled workers who maintain the textile and garment standards for Canada’s hospitality, healthcare, and retail sectors. As a TEER 5 occupation, this role is the operational backbone of commercial laundries, hotel linen services, and specialized dry-cleaning establishments. In 2026, as Canada’s tourism and institutional healthcare sectors expand to record levels, laundry and dry-cleaning professionals are in high demand to ensure sanitary and aesthetic excellence. For immigration, this role is a frequent target for the Atlantic Immigration Program (AIP) and Provincial Nominee Programs (PNPs) that prioritize essential service support.

classification.teer: Classification LevelDetailsBroad Occupation Category6 – Sales and service occupationsTEER Level5Major Group65 – Sales and service support occupationsSub-Major Group653 – Support occupations in personal and specialized servicesMinor Group6532 – Dry cleaning and laundry support occupationsNOC Code65320

duties: Operate dry cleaning and laundry machines to clean garments, linens, and other textile articles.
Sort and inspect articles according to color, fabric type, and cleaning requirements.
Apply specialized chemical treatments to remove spots and stains from garments before or after cleaning.
Operate industrial pressing machines or hand irons to finish garments and linens to professional standards.
Perform minor repairs on garments, such as replacing buttons or mending small tears.
Package and label cleaned items for delivery or customer pickup.
Monitor and maintain chemical levels and equipment performance to ensure safety and quality.
employment_requirements: Some secondary school education is usually required.
On-the-job training is provided for the operation of specialized machinery and the handling of cleaning chemicals.
WHMIS Certification: Mandatory for all workers handling industrial detergents and dry-cleaning solvents.
Physical stamina and the ability to work in warm, fast-paced environments are essential.
Basic proficiency in English or French is required for safety coordination and following specific garment care instructions.
job_outlook: The outlook for this occupation is Favourable. Projections indicate over 10,000 new job openings nationally due to industry growth and the replacement of workers moving into supervisory roles such as Cleaning Supervisors (NOC 62024). The expansion of "eco-friendly" dry cleaning services is also creating new niches for workers trained in modern, non-toxic cleaning technologies.
labour_market_demand: The demand for laundry and dry-cleaning workers is Good, particularly in provinces with large urban hospitality hubs and extensive healthcare networks. In 2026, there is a noted surge in demand in Ontario, British Columbia, and Quebec. As hotels return to full capacity and hospitals expand their facilities, the requirement for high-volume textile processing has created a steady stream of job openings for international workers.
Technical Skill Acquisition: Gain experience operating industrial-grade cleaning machinery and specialized chemical treatment systems.
Essential Service Stability: Work in a recession-proof sector that provides vital support to the healthcare and hospitality industries.
Flexible Environments: Choose from roles in high-volume industrial plants, boutique dry cleaners, or in-house hotel laundry departments.
Direct Path to PR: A recognized occupation for "Semi-Skilled" immigration streams across multiple provinces.
pathways: Atlantic Immigration Program (AIP): A high-conversion route for workers with job offers from designated employers in the Atlantic region (NS, NB, PEI, NL).
BC PNP Entry-Level and Semi-Skilled (ELSS): Accessible for those working in the hospitality or service sector for 9 consecutive months in BC.
Alberta Opportunity Stream: Fast-track PR for those currently working in Alberta in eligible service categories.
Manitoba Skilled Worker Stream: Targeted at workers who have been employed in the province for at least 6 months.
